title: Rare Earth Magnet Supply Crisis — DoD-Anchored US Capacity Comes Online
catalyst: China's April 2025 export controls on seven heavy rare earths, related compounds, and finished magnets remain in force and have created persistent shortages — US magnet imports fell 11% YoY in November 2025, and automakers in the US and Europe were forced to cut utilization rates or temporarily idle plants. Beijing's November 2025 'pause' applied only to the October 2025 second wave and runs through November 2026, leaving the original April controls untouched. Against that backdrop, MP Materials' Independence (Texas) magnet facility comes online in 2026 with 1,000 MT of initial capacity under a 10-year DoD off-take agreement covering 100% of output.
thesis: China still controls roughly 60% of rare earth mining and ~91% of refining/separation, so end customers — defense primes, EV makers, robotics OEMs, wind turbine builders — are being forced to pay structural premiums for any non-China supply. The market is treating MP as a pre-revenue story, but 2026 is when the mine-to-magnet thesis actually starts producing salable magnets, with the Pentagon as both anchor customer and largest shareholder (~$400M preferred, July 2025). Combined with US defense magnet demand projected to hit 5,000 MT by 2028 and US e-mobility magnet demand projected at 145,000 MT by 2030, MP's incremental tonnage is effectively pre-sold at premium prices long before competitive Western capacity exists.

ticker_rationale.MP: MP Materials is the only integrated US mine-to-magnet rare earth producer — it operates Mountain Pass (the only US rare earth mine), refining/separation in California, and the Independence magnet facility in Fort Worth that begins commercial production in 2026. Revenue grew 70% YoY heading into the magnet ramp, the DoD became the largest shareholder via a $400M preferred investment in July 2025, and the DoD has committed to ensure 100% of 10X Facility output is purchased by defense and commercial customers for 10 years post-construction. The 2028 ramp to ~10,000 MT total US magnet capacity provides a multi-year volume + price growth runway with a sovereign customer underwriting demand.

risks: China could flood global markets with rare earths to suppress prices and undermine MP's path to magnet profitability — a tactic Beijing has used in prior cycles
Magnet manufacturing execution risk — no Western company has scaled commercial NdFeB magnet production in decades; first-year yields and throughput at Independence could disappoint
A US–China trade détente or formal lifting of the April 2025 export controls would normalize magnet supply and compress the premium pricing thesis
MP trades at ~40x forward P/E — much of the strategic-asset premium is already embedded in the stock, so execution misses on the magnet ramp would trigger sharp re-rating
Internal culture concerns (Glassdoor 2.2/5, 31% recommend) could impair talent retention during the most operationally complex phase of the company's history
summary: China's rare earth export controls have created persistent supply shortages, and MP Materials' DoD-anchored Independence magnet facility is the first Western mine-to-magnet operation to come online, with a 10-year off-take agreement and a path to 10,000 MT US capacity by 2028.
body_md:
## Setup

China controls approximately 60% of global rare earth mining and 91% of refining and separation capacity, giving Beijing an effective chokepoint over the materials that power everything from F-35 fighter jets to EV motors to wind turbines [1][2]. In April 2025, China imposed export controls on seven heavy rare earths, related compounds, and finished NdFeB magnets — the most strategically important permanent magnet material. A second wave of controls in October 2025 extended restrictions to products containing even 0.1% Chinese-origin rare earth content by value, applying the foreign direct product rule (FDPR) for the first time [2].

The impact was immediate. US magnet imports fell 11% YoY by November 2025. Chinese customs data show yttrium exports to the US collapsed from 333 tons in the eight months before the April controls to just 17 tons in the eight months after [2]. Automakers in the US and Europe cut utilization rates or temporarily idled plants due to magnet shortages. Beijing's November 2025 "pause" on the second wave of controls (running through November 2026) provided some relief, but the original April controls remain in force, maintaining structural supply constraints [2].

## Why Now

The critical inflection is MP Materials' transition from a pre-revenue magnet story to an actual commercial producer. The Independence facility in Fort Worth, Texas, achieved first alloy flake and finished magnet production on commercial equipment in 2025, restoring end-to-end rare earth production capabilities in the United States for the first time in decades [3]. In February 2026, MP selected a 120-acre site in Northlake, Texas (less than 10 miles from Independence) for the "10X" campus, which will scale total US magnet capacity to an estimated 10,000 metric tonnes upon commissioning in 2028 [4].

The DoD partnership fundamentally de-risks the demand side. In July 2025, the Department of Defense became MP's largest shareholder through a ~$400M preferred investment and entered a 10-year agreement establishing a price floor of $110 per kilogram for MP's NdPr products [3]. For the 10X facility, DoD has committed to ensure 100% of output is purchased by defense and commercial customers for 10 years post-construction — effectively pre-selling the entire capacity before a single magnet is produced [3].

This demand underwriting comes at a time when US defense magnet requirements are projected to hit 5,000 MT by 2028 and US e-mobility magnet demand is forecast at 145,000 MT by 2030 [1]. MP's incremental tonnage cannot meet even a fraction of total demand, giving the company pricing power that grows with each tonne of new capacity.

## Company-Specific Edge

MP Materials (MP) is the only fully integrated mine-to-magnet rare earth producer in the Western Hemisphere. The company operates Mountain Pass — the only active rare earth mine in the United States — along with refining and separation operations in California and the Independence/10X magnet facilities in Texas [5].

The transformation from Molycorp's bankruptcy to America's strategic rare earth champion has been remarkable. Revenue grew 70% YoY heading into the magnet ramp, and Q4 2025 EPS of $0.09 beat consensus estimates by 350% [5]. The company severed its dependency on Chinese partner Shenghe, achieving in-house separation at Mountain Pass — a technically challenging milestone that validates the downstream manufacturing capability. With $1.83 billion in cash against $1.03 billion in debt (D/E 43.2%), MP has substantial balance sheet runway to complete the 10X buildout [5].

The DoD relationship provides not just demand but strategic insulation. As the Pentagon's largest rare earth investment, MP benefits from policy support, potential additional appropriations, and priority access to government contracting channels. J.P. Morgan led the transformational financing, underscoring institutional credibility [3].

## Numbers That Matter

**MP Materials (MP)**:
- Revenue: $224M TTM, growing 70% YoY
- Net loss: -$85.9M (FY2025), improving 31% YoY
- EPS path: consensus $0.71 (2026), $1.25 (2027) — profitability inflection
- Valuation: 40.4x forward P/E, D/E 43.2%
- Cash: $1.83B vs. $1.03B debt — ample runway
- DoD price floor: $110/kg for NdPr products (10-year agreement)
- Capacity ramp: ~1,000 MT (Independence, 2026) to ~10,000 MT (10X, 2028)
- Market cap: ~$9.2B on expectations of full mine-to-magnet value capture

**Supply context**:
- China controls ~91% of rare earth refining/separation
- US magnet imports: -11% YoY (Nov 2025)
- Chinese yttrium exports to US: 333 tons -> 17 tons (April-Dec 2025)
- US defense magnet demand: projected 5,000 MT by 2028

## Bear Case

China retains the ability to flood global rare earth markets at any time to suppress prices and undermine Western producers' economics. Beijing used this tactic in 2010-2011, driving prices to artificially low levels that bankrupted Molycorp (MP's predecessor). A repeat strategy targeting the NdPr oxide market could make MP's $110/kg DoD price floor look attractive relative to spot but compress the premium pricing thesis for commercial sales.

Magnet manufacturing execution risk is significant. No Western company has successfully scaled commercial NdFeB magnet production in decades, and the transition from pilot-scale to commercial-scale involves yield, quality, and throughput challenges that cannot be fully anticipated [5]. First-year production at Independence may underwhelm expectations.

At ~40x forward P/E with negative current earnings, the stock prices in successful execution of the full mine-to-magnet vision. Any delays in the 10X commissioning timeline (targeted 2028) or DoD contract execution would trigger significant multiple compression. The 52-week range of $18.64 to $100.25 reflects extreme investor uncertainty.

Internal culture is a genuine concern. Glassdoor ratings of 2.2/5 culture and 31% recommend rate at a 998-person company suggest organizational challenges that could impair talent retention during the most operationally complex phase of the company's history [5].

Finally, a US-China trade détente or formal lifting of the April 2025 export controls would normalize magnet supply and compress the strategic premium that underpins MP's valuation.

## Conclusion

The rare earth magnet thesis is a strategic supply chain opportunity anchored in geopolitical reality: China controls 91% of rare earth refining, the original April 2025 export controls remain in force, and MP Materials is the only Western company with a credible mine-to-magnet operation. The DoD partnership de-risks demand with a 10-year off-take agreement and $110/kg price floor, while the 10X campus ramp to 10,000 MT by 2028 provides a multi-year growth runway. The key monitoring points are Independence facility production yields, 10X construction milestones, China export control policy changes, and MP's path to positive earnings (consensus $0.71 EPS in 2026).
invalidation: China formally lifts the April 2025 rare earth export controls AND resumes unrestricted NdFeB magnet exports, normalizing global supply and collapsing the Western premium pricing thesis
MP Materials' Independence facility produces less than 500 MT of salable magnets in its first 12 months of commercial operation, signaling fundamental manufacturing execution failure
NdPr oxide prices fall below $80/kg for two consecutive months, making the DoD's $110/kg price floor the only economically viable off-take channel
